network.overall_strategy: The network centers glutamate synthesis on the literature-established α-ketoglutarate hub while preserving additional experimentally observed low-selectivity background routes. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced C1-C3 feedstocks through Fe0/FeS/greigite/magnetite catalysis and mild vent conditions rather than high-temperature black-smoker settings, consistent with the literature that glutamate is unstable at high temperature. Surface chemistry contributes HCN, acrolein, atmospheric discharge products, oligomer pools, and concentration processes under UV, wet-dry, and mineral-assisted settings. Biochemical/prebiotic modules then convert α-ketoglutarate to glutamate by multiple experimentally demonstrated routes: alum-catalyzed transamination, NADH-mediated reductive amination, H2/Ni reductive amination, FeS/H2S reductive amination, pyrite-photocatalyzed reductive amination, and Fe0/hydroxylamine-promoted protometabolic emergence. Ten pathways are encoded, with α-ketoglutarate, pyruvate, glyoxylate, HCN, and glutamate aminonitrile serving as the main hubs. Speculative or weakly selective branches are explicitly labeled as such, and the known one-pot failure for glutamate accumulation is reflected by using compartmentalized pathways rather than a single integrated route.

reactions.name: Fe0-driven CO2 reduction to formate, acetate, and pyruvate

conditions.catalyst: elemental iron with iron-sulfur mineral assistance
conditions.other: anoxic, mild hydrothermal conditions; reduced vent fluids

reactions.mechanism: Native iron transfers electrons to CO2, generating C1-C3 reduced carboxylates through surface-mediated carbon fixation chemistry analogous to acetyl-CoA pathway logic.
reactions.reasoning: Varma et al. (2018) showed Fe0 reduces CO2 to formate, acetate, and pyruvate at 30-100°C in water. Inclusion of greigite is consistent with hydrothermal Fe-S catalysis and CO2 reduction chemistry documented for Hadean vent minerals by Preiner et al. (2020), Roldan et al. (2015), and White et al. (2015). This reaction provides the hydrothermal reduced-carbon module feeding later surface and prebiotic steps.

reactions.name: Magnetite/borate-assisted surface generation and concentration of glyoxylate

conditions.other: UV-exposed shallow pools; step added to connect carbon modules; concentration by evaporation/freezing is required

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical oxidation/rearrangement of reduced C1 feedstocks in surface pools yields small aldehyde-acid intermediates, with borate helping preserve labile oxygenated carbon species.
reactions.reasoning: The supplied literature does not provide a single definitive prebiotic glyoxylate source but identifies concentration of carbonyl precursors as an open problem. This node is therefore explicitly a necessary connecting step, not a claimed direct literature-demonstrated glutamate route. It is constrained to plausible surface photochemistry and stabilization rather than invented downstream chemistry. Use of TiO2 and borate follows general prebiotic photochemical and sugar/carbonyl stabilization frameworks (Patel et al., 2015; Cleaves, 2014, as cited in the review for concentration challenges).

reactions.name: Fe2+-promoted protometabolic condensation of pyruvate and glyoxylate to alpha-ketoglutarate

conditions.other: anoxic warm aqueous conditions; approximately 3 h network timescale in the reported system

reactions.mechanism: Aldol-type carbon-carbon bond formation and protometabolic rearrangements within an iron-promoted TCA-like network convert C3 and C2 ketoacids into the C5 ketoacid alpha-ketoglutarate.
reactions.reasoning: Muchowska et al. (2019) demonstrated Fe2+-promoted synthesis of 9 of 11 TCA intermediates, including alpha-ketoglutarate, from pyruvate and glyoxylate at 70°C. This is the most central literature-supported precursor step for glutamate and is treated as the main convergent hub-forming process.

reactions.name: Metal-free pyruvate-glyoxylate network to alpha-ketoglutarate
reactions.mechanism: Metal-free aldol chemistry and Cannizzaro-type redox steps convert pyruvate and glyoxylate into higher TCA-like intermediates including alpha-ketoglutarate.
reactions.reasoning: Stubbs et al. (2020) established a metal-free glyoxylate-pyruvate protometabolic network at pH 4.5 and 70°C that produces alpha-ketoglutarate analogs/intermediates. This provides an independent convergent route to the alpha-ketoglutarate hub.

reactions.name: Alum-catalyzed transamination of alpha-ketoglutarate with glycine to glutamate
reactions.mechanism: Nonenzymatic transamination transfers the amino group from glycine-derived donor chemistry onto alpha-ketoglutarate, yielding glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: Stubbs et al. (2020) reported 44% glutamate yield from transamination of alpha-ketoglutarate with glycine using AlK(SO4)2 at pH 5 and 80°C over 4 h. This is one of the best-supported and highest-yield prebiotic glutamate syntheses.

reactions.name: NADH-mediated reductive amination of alpha-ketoglutarate
reactions.mechanism: Imine formation between alpha-ketoglutarate and ammonia is followed by hydride transfer from NADH to produce glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: Nogal et al. (2024) obtained 33% glutamate through NADH-mediated reductive amination of alpha-ketoglutarate at room temperature and pH above 7. The review specifically identifies this as a demonstrated prebiotic route, though upstream NADH availability remains conditional.

reactions.name: H2-driven nickel-catalyzed reductive amination of alpha-ketoglutarate

conditions.other: pH 8; individual step works but integrated one-pot system fails to accumulate glutamate

reactions.mechanism: Nickel activates H2 for reduction of the alpha-ketoglutarate/ammonia imine intermediate to glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: Kaur et al. (2024) demonstrated H2-driven reductive amination of alpha-ketoglutarate to glutamate over nickel catalysts at 22°C, pH 8, and 5 bar H2, including activity from ground Aletai meteorite. The same study showed glutamate was not detectable in the full one-pot integrated network, so this step is encoded only as a separated module.

reactions.name: FeS/H2S-mediated hydrothermal reductive amination of alpha-ketoglutarate

conditions.other: anoxic hydrothermal conditions; modeled on alpha-keto acid amination

reactions.mechanism: FeS/H2S surfaces promote imine formation and reduction of alpha-keto acids to amino acids under warm hydrothermal conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Hafenbradl et al. (1995) demonstrated FeS/H2S-driven reductive amination of alpha-keto acids to amino acids at 100°C. The review identifies this chemistry as directly applicable to alpha-ketoglutarate-to-glutamate conversion. Using FeS and pyrite conforms to hydrothermal mineral requirements while staying within the reported iron-sulfur framework.

reactions.name: Pyrite-photocatalyzed reductive amination of alpha-ketoglutarate

conditions.other: surface photochemical setting; strong UV irradiation; TiO2 included as a co-photocatalytic mineral context

reactions.mechanism: UV-excited pyrite surface electrons drive reductive amination of alpha-keto acids to amino acids at mineral-water interfaces.
reactions.reasoning: The review cites a 2024 Nature Communications report of UV-irradiated natural pyrite driving reductive amination of alpha-keto acids to amino acids including glutamate, with measurable enantiomeric excesses. This provides a surface-specific, mineral-mediated route from the alpha-ketoglutarate hub.

reactions.name: Fe0 and hydroxylamine promoted protometabolic emergence of glutamate from pyruvate and glyoxylate

conditions.other: anoxic warm aqueous protometabolic network; glutamate appears within a complex product set

reactions.mechanism: Protometabolic carbon-carbon bond formation generates alpha-ketoglutarate while reduced nitrogen species enable amino acid appearance within the network, producing glutamate as an emergent member of the product ensemble.
reactions.reasoning: Muchowska et al. (2019) reported that adding hydroxylamine and Fe0 to the pyruvate-glyoxylate Fe2+-promoted network generated four biological amino acids including glutamate. This is encoded as a low-selectivity but literature-supported emergence route rather than a clean single-step synthesis.

reactions.name: Spark discharge atmospheric synthesis delivering HCN, acrolein, and glycine

conditions.other: electric discharge over reduced gas mixtures; collection in aqueous condensate; includes volcanic/H2S-rich variants

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge in reduced atmospheres generates nitriles, aldehydes, and amino acids that are trapped in condensate and concentrated on mineral surfaces.
reactions.reasoning: Miller (1955), Ring et al. (1972), Johnson et al. (2008), and Parker et al. (2011) documented glutamate and related organics in spark-discharge mixtures derived from reduced gases, not from ammonia alone. Acrolein is specifically implicated as a glutamate precursor by Van Trump and Miller (1972) and Parker et al. (2011). This reaction intentionally uses proper atmospheric feed gases to avoid the error noted in prior attempts.

reactions.name: Acrolein-based Strecker/Michael route to glutamate aminonitrile

conditions.other: aqueous surface pools; HCN-rich localized conditions; Michael addition followed by Strecker-type amination

reactions.mechanism: Acrolein first undergoes conjugate addition by cyanide, then Strecker-type reaction with ammonia and cyanide to generate the glutamate aminonitrile precursor pool.
reactions.reasoning: Van Trump and Miller (1972) identified acrolein as the key precursor for glutamate Strecker chemistry, and Parker et al. (2011) confirmed this mechanism in reanalyzed Miller samples. The review notes this route as classical and experimentally grounded, but also highlights the open issue of precursor concentration. This reaction therefore encodes the established middle of the pathway without inventing unsupported succinaldehyde synthesis.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of glutamate aminonitrile to glutamate

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis during prolonged warm wet phases

reactions.mechanism: Sequential nitrile hydrolysis converts the aminonitrile intermediate into the dicarboxylic amino acid glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of Strecker aminonitriles to amino acids is standard in the classical prebiotic framework and is the endpoint of the acrolein-based glutamate route discussed by Van Trump and Miller (1972) and Parker et al. (2011). This step is represented separately to avoid over-compression.

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to DAMN-rich precursor pool

conditions.other: requires concentrated HCN, approximately >=0.01 M; concentration by eutectic freezing or evaporation

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomerizes into aminomalononitrile/DAMN-rich polymeric pools under concentrated conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Ferris et al. (1978) showed HCN oligomerization at 0.1 M and pH 9.2 produces complex oligomeric material that upon hydrolysis releases glutamate among many products. Sanchez et al. (1966) support eutectic freezing as a concentration mechanism. DAMN is used here as a representative discrete node for the oligomer pool to avoid an unrealistic direct HCN-to-glutamate jump.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomer pool to glutamate and glycine

conditions.other: post-oligomer hydrolysis; low selectivity, diffuse product distribution

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic breakdown of HCN oligomer/polymer mixtures releases amino acids as minor products, including glutamate and glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Ferris et al. (1978) and Moser and Matthews (1968) reported glutamic acid from hydrolysis of HCN-derived oligomers and aminoacetonitrile polymers. This route is explicitly treated as low-selectivity background chemistry rather than a targeted synthesis, addressing prior criticism.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photochemistry to acrylonitrile precursor pool

conditions.catalyst: UV photoredox with sulfide mineral context
conditions.other: HCN/H2S/UV cyanosulfidic setting; precursor-pool chemistry rather than isolated high-yield transformation

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven cyanosulfidic networks transform simple nitrile feedstocks into more advanced nitriles such as acrylonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) established cyanosulfidic chemistry from HCN and H2S under photoredox conditions, and Kuroda and Kobayashi (2024) discuss acrylonitrile in networks leading to glutamine or glutamic acid precursors. This branch is included as literature-inspired but less direct than alpha-ketoglutarate routes.

reactions.name: Acrylonitrile-derived precursor conversion to glutamate aminonitrile

conditions.catalyst: surface mineral concentration and nitrile activation
conditions.other: encoded as precursor-pool conversion; lower confidence than classical Strecker route

reactions.mechanism: Conjugate addition and nitrile-functionalization chemistry within cyanosulfidic nitrile pools yields glutamate-related aminonitrile precursors.
reactions.reasoning: Kuroda and Kobayashi (2024) support acrylonitrile-derived glutamine/glutamic-acid precursor chemistry in cyanosulfidic contexts. Because the exact stepwise sequence remains less directly demonstrated than the acrolein route, this reaction is intentionally framed as a lower-confidence precursor-pool conversion rather than a clean isolated transformation.

reactions.name: Mild hydrothermal generation of HCN from atmospheric/surface delivery and concentration

conditions.other: UV-exposed atmospheric condensates; included to make HCN a reusable hub in surface modules

reactions.mechanism: Atmospheric photochemistry/discharge produces cyanide species that are trapped and concentrated on mineral surfaces in shallow waters.
reactions.reasoning: Sagan and Khare (1971) and Miller-era discharge studies support atmospheric generation of nitrile-rich product mixtures under UV or spark conditions. This node separates HCN availability from downstream oligomerization and Strecker steps, improving network granularity and avoiding compressed chemistry criticized in previous attempts.

reactions.name: Surface formation of hydroxylamine/nitrogen oxyanion reduction products

conditions.other: included as a connector for the Muchowska amino-acid-emergence network

reactions.mechanism: Surface redox chemistry generates reduced nitrogen intermediates including hydroxylamine that can enter protometabolic amination networks.
reactions.reasoning: Muchowska et al. (2019) specifically used hydroxylamine as a nitrogen source in the Fe0-promoted protometabolic amino-acid-generating network. The review also discusses nitrogen reduction challenges and Fe/S-mediated reductions (Summers and Chang, 1993). This step is included conservatively as a connector required for the demonstrated downstream chemistry.

pathways.name: Fe2+-protometabolic alpha-ketoglutarate then alum transamination
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction supplies pyruvate; surface processes provide glyoxylate; Fe2+-promoted TCA-like chemistry generates alpha-ketoglutarate, which is transaminated to glutamate with the highest reported yield.

pathways.name: Metal-free alpha-ketoglutarate then alum transamination
pathways.description: A metal-free pyruvate-glyoxylate network forms alpha-ketoglutarate, followed by alum-catalyzed transamination to glutamate.

pathways.name: Fe2+-protometabolic alpha-ketoglutarate then NADH reductive amination
pathways.description: Alpha-ketoglutarate produced in a TCA-like network is aminated under mild basic conditions using NADH as hydride donor.

pathways.name: Fe2+-protometabolic alpha-ketoglutarate then H2/Ni reductive amination
pathways.description: Separated from one-pot protometabolism, alpha-ketoglutarate is converted to glutamate over nickel under hydrogen pressure.

pathways.name: Fe2+-protometabolic alpha-ketoglutarate then FeS/H2S hydrothermal amination
pathways.description: Alpha-ketoglutarate is formed prebiotically and then aminated in a warm iron-sulfur hydrothermal setting.

pathways.name: Fe2+-protometabolic alpha-ketoglutarate then pyrite-photocatalyzed amination
pathways.description: The alpha-ketoglutarate hub is converted to glutamate at UV-irradiated pyrite-rich surface interfaces.

pathways.name: Protometabolic emergence with hydroxylamine and Fe0
pathways.description: Glutamate emerges directly within the pyruvate-glyoxylate protometabolic network when Fe0 and hydroxylamine are added, while alpha-ketoglutarate also appears.

pathways.name: Classical acrolein-based Strecker route
pathways.description: Atmospheric discharge produces acrolein and HCN; acrolein undergoes Michael/Strecker chemistry to glutamate aminonitrile, which hydrolyzes to glutamate.

pathways.name: HCN oligomerization and hydrolysis background route
pathways.description: Concentrated HCN oligomerizes to DAMN-rich polymer pools that release glutamate upon hydrolysis as a low-selectivity side product.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic acrylonitrile-to-aminonitrile route
pathways.description: Surface cyanosulfidic chemistry generates acrylonitrile-type precursors that are converted into glutamate-related aminonitrile pools before hydrolysis to glutamate.
